generations.text:
My Dearest Eliza,

I write to you now with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. The wind howls like a wounded beast outside these walls, and the sea roars as though it too mourns the distance between us. I have not seen you in five years, and yet, in this moment of impending peril, I find myself thinking of you more than I have in a lifetime.

You were but a child when you left, and I, in my pride and folly, allowed you to go. I have since been a solitary sentinel of stone and flame, watching the world pass in a blur of waves and stars. But tonight—tonight, the heavens themselves seem to weep. The storm is coming, and I fear it may take more than the light from this tower.

I do not know if I shall survive it, Eliza. The sea has taken many before me, and I am no longer the man I was. My body grows frail, my vision blurs, and my hands, once steady in their duty, now shake with the weight of time and regret. Yet I have kept the light burning, not for the glory of it, but for the hope that perhaps, somewhere out there, someone still watches.

I have not written to you in five years, and I have not spoken of you in the way a father should. But I am writing now, not as a man who has forgotten his child, but as one who has

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like the very devil himself outside these walls. The sea rages with a fury I have not seen since the day you left. I pray the ink holds, and that the letter finds you, wherever you may be.

Five long years have passed since you walked out of this house, your face a mask of sorrow, your voice a whisper of disappointment. I have not spoken your name in all that time, and yet it lingers in my thoughts like the echo of a storm. I have tried, my child, I have tried to forgive. But forgiveness is not so easily given when the heart is carved with pain.

You were once my light, Eliza. The bright, golden hope of my weary soul. I used to think that with you, I would never be alone. But you left, not with a shout, nor with a tear, but with a silence so deep it cut through me like a blade. You did not say goodbye. You did not say *I love you*. You simply vanished, as though you had never been.

I have kept the lantern burning, as I always have. Night after night, through the bitter cold and the endless rain, I have stood at the window, watching the waves crash against the rocks. The light has guided countless ships to safety, but I have never seen one of them return to me. I have given my life to this place

My Dearest Clara,

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee at the window, rattling the shutters and sending shivers down my spine. The storm is upon us, and though I have stood through many tempests in my years as a lighthouse keeper, this one feels different—more fierce, more relentless. I fear I may not see the dawn.

But it is not the storm that has brought me to pen these words, though it is a fitting backdrop for a letter that must be sent. Five years have passed since our last conversation, and I have not spoken to you in that time. I have been a man of silence, a keeper of light, but not of your heart. I know the guilt that lingers in my soul, and I cannot bear to let it fester any longer.

You were so young when you left, Clara. Just a girl, with dreams of the world beyond these shores. I remember the day you walked out of our home, your eyes filled with determination, your heart heavy with the weight of unspoken words. You said you needed to find yourself, to chase the horizon, and I, in my pride and fear, allowed you to go. I thought it was for the best. I thought it would be better for you.

But the years have not been kind to that decision. I have not seen you, nor heard from you, and the silence has grown heavier with each passing day

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. The storm howls outside like the very devil himself, and I fear the winds may take me before I can see your face again. Five years, Eliza. Five years since you left me, and five years since I've spoken your name without bitterness.

I have not forgotten you. Not a single day has passed without the memory of your laughter echoing in the hollow of this lighthouse. You were my light, my reason for rising with the dawn, and now, as the sky darkens and the sea roars its fury, I find myself wondering if I have been a fool to keep this light burning for you.

The men who came to check on the lighthouse said the lamp still works, that the beacon remains steady. They said I should not be alone. But I have always been alone, Eliza. Even when you were here, even when your arms were around me, I carried the weight of our silence. You left with your father's words, and I stood here, a broken man, waiting for a daughter who no longer called me *father*.

I know you must have thought I was cruel, that I had turned my back on you. But I was not cruel. I was afraid. Afraid that if I let you in, I would lose you again. I was afraid that the love I held for you would be the very

My dearest Eliza,

I sit here in the dim glow of the oil lamp, the wind howling like a banshee outside the lighthouse. The sea is restless, and the sky is black as a storm-tossed grave. I’ve watched the waves grow taller with every passing hour, and I fear this night may be the last I see of the world. But if I should perish, I wish to leave you with something more than silence.

Five years have passed since you left, and I have not written a single word to you. I told myself it would be easier this way—no more letters, no more arguments, no more heartache. But the silence has grown heavy, like the weight of the sea pressing against these stone walls. I’ve kept the light burning every night, as I always have, but tonight, I fear it may not be enough.

I remember the day you left. You stood at the door, your face pale, your eyes filled with something I could not name. You said you were going to find your own path, and I said nothing. I thought if I said nothing, it would be easier for you. But I was wrong. I have not been the same since.

You were my light, Eliza. You were the reason I kept this lighthouse, the reason I rose before dawn to tend the flame. You were my heart, and now I have only the sea and the wind to speak for me.

I have no

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write to you now with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken. The sea, as ever, roars in fury, and the wind howls like a wounded beast. Tonight, a storm of such ferocity I have not seen in my lifetime is upon us. I fear for my life, and in that fear, I am reminded of the silence that has settled between us—silence that has grown like a shadow over these years.

Five long years have passed since the last time we spoke, and I have often wondered if you still remember the sound of my voice, or if the memories of our past have faded as the tides recede. I have kept the lighthouse as you once asked, and I have stood guard through many a storm, but none have ever felt as dire as this. The sea is restless, and the stars, which have always been my companions, seem to weep with me.

I know you must have thought me cruel, perhaps even unkind, in the years that have passed. I was wrong to let pride and sorrow drive me away. I was not the man I once was, and I do not deserve the love and forgiveness you once gave me. But I have come to understand that love is not measured in words, but in the quiet moments of a father's heart.

I have not forgotten the days we spent by the shore, when you were but a child and

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with sorrow, for I know not how long I may live to see the dawn. The sea, that fickle and cruel mistress, has summoned a storm such as I have not seen in many a year. The wind howls like the wails of the damned, and the waves, like vengeful spirits, crash upon the rocks with a fury that strikes fear into the very soul of the lighthouse.

I have been here, in this lonely tower, for many years, and though I have grown used to the solitude, there is a part of me that yearns for the warmth of your voice, for the sound of your laughter, for the sight of your face. I have not spoken to you in five years, and though I have tried to forgive, I know not if I have done so. You left without a word, without a farewell, and I have carried that silence like a stone upon my heart.

I have always been a man of duty, of steadfastness, and I have tried to live by those virtues. But I have also been a fool, Eleanor, and I have let pride and pride alone stand between us. I have not written, nor have I sent a message, and I have told myself that I would not be the one to reach out first. But now, as the storm gathers strength and the sky darkens with an ominous cloud, I find myself thinking

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

It is with a heart both heavy and hopeful that I take up my quill this evening, as the wind howls like the very souls of the sea and the sky is black as the deepest abyss. I have not written to you in five years, a span that feels like an eternity in the eyes of a man who once believed in the constancy of the stars. Yet, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself compelled to reach out—to you, my only child, and to the memories that bind us.

I have often thought of you, Eleanor. Not in the manner of a man who seeks to rekindle a relationship, but in the quiet moments when the sea is calm and the light shines steady upon the waves. You were a child of the light, always seeking it, always yearning for more. I wonder what you have become. Have you found your own light? Or have you, like so many, been blinded by the glare of the world?

I must confess, I have not spoken of the storm. I have not told you that the winds have been growing stronger, that the sea has grown restless, and that the very air seems to hum with the promise of destruction. I do not know if I shall see the dawn. But if I do, I shall not be the

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken. The wind howls like a wounded beast outside, and the sea roars as if it too mourns the silence between us. I have not seen you in five years, not so much as a letter or a word from you, and now I sit here, pen in hand, knowing that perhaps I may not live to see the dawn.

I have spent these years watching the waves, listening to the cries of the gulls, and keeping the light burning for those who sail the dark waters. It has been a lonely task, but one I have borne with the hope that, someday, I might be able to look upon the horizon and see you again.

I have not written to you before, not because I lacked the courage, but because I feared what might come of it. I feared that my words would be met with silence, or worse, with scorn. And yet, as the storm gathers strength and the sky darkens with the promise of tempest, I find myself compelled to speak, to tell you the truth I have kept locked away for too long.

I have not forgotten you, Eleanor. Not a single day has passed without me thinking of you, of the laughter we once shared, of the dreams we once dared to dream. I have tried to live my life with purpose, with honor, and with the hope that one day, you might

**Letter from the Lighthouse Keeper to His Estranged Daughter**

*April 12th, 1889*

My Dearest Eleanor,

I hope this letter finds you well, though I dares not imagine what you might be doing in that city of yours. Five years have passed since you left, and I have not heard a word from you. I suppose that is what happens when one chooses to forget. You were always the one who wanted more than the sea could offer, and I, perhaps, was too stubborn to let you go.

The storm is coming. The wind howls like a beast with no mercy, and the sea is rising. I’ve spent my life watching the horizon, but tonight, I fear it may be the last time I do so. The lighthouse has stood for over a century, but even stone can break beneath the weight of the elements. I’ve lit the beacon every night, as I always have, but tonight, I feel the light may not be enough.

I’ve thought of you often, more than I care to admit. You were my only child, my light in the darkness. I remember the nights you’d come to the lighthouse as a child, wide-eyed with wonder, asking questions about the stars and the waves. You had such a mind, Eleanor—curious, restless, and full of dreams. I tried to guide you, but you always wanted to go your own way. I was wrong to hold

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**April 12th, 1893**

I write this with a trembling hand and a heart heavier than the storm that brews on the horizon. Five years have passed since you left, and yet the weight of your absence clings to me like the salt of the sea. I have not spoken your name in all that time, not even in the silence of the lighthouse, where the wind howls and the waves crash like the cries of a soul in torment.

The night is dark, and the sea is restless. I have seen the sky turn the color of blood, and the wind whispers of doom. The lamps are lit, as they must be, but I fear they may not be enough. The storm is coming, and I am not certain I will see dawn.

I have no words for the pain of your departure, no explanation that would soothe the wound you left in my soul. You said I was too harsh, too strict, too much of a man of the sea. But I was only trying to protect you, as I have always tried. I thought the world was dangerous, and that you would be safer far from this place, far from the cold and the dark. I thought I was doing the right thing. I was wrong.

I have not seen your face in a mirror for years, not since the day you left with that man. I do not know if he is still there,

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may find you in sorrow, for I write it with a heart heavy with regret and longing. Five years have passed since you left this house, and though the years have worn down the walls, they have not softened the ache of your absence. I have not spoken to you in that time, and I do not know if you would even wish to hear from me now. But tonight, as the wind howls like a wounded beast and the sea roars its fury, I feel the weight of silence pressing upon me, and I must try to speak, if only to the wind and the waves.

The storm is coming, Eleanor. A great, terrible storm, as if the heavens themselves are crying out in wrath. The sky is dark as pitch, and the sea, which I have watched for so many years, now seems to rise in defiance. I have lit the lamp, as I always do, though I fear it may not be enough to guide the ships that may come seeking shelter in the night. I have no doubt that the light will shine, as it always has, but I wonder if it will be enough to save anyone—or if it will be too late.

I have often thought of you in these moments, when the wind howls and the waves crash against the rocks. I have imagined you standing in the world beyond this lonely shore, perhaps with a husband and children,

My Dearest Eliza,

I write to you with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken. It has been five long years since I last set eyes upon you, and though the sea has borne witness to many a storm, none have stirred within me the tempest of longing that now consumes me.

The night is dark, the skies are black as ink, and the wind howls like a banshee in the distance. The waves, once gentle and predictable, now rage with a fury that makes me question whether I shall see dawn. I have spent my life guarding this lighthouse, guiding ships through the treacherous waters, but tonight, I fear I may not be able to guide myself through this storm.

I have not written to you in years, and I know you may not wish to hear from me. I have been a fool, Eliza, a man who let pride and silence stand between us. I have not been the father you deserved, nor the man you once believed in. But I write now not with the hope of absolution, but with the hope that you might hear the truth that has been buried beneath years of silence.

The storm is coming, and I am not certain I shall survive it. I have always been the strong one, the one who stood firm against the elements, but tonight, I feel the wind of fate pressing against my ribs. I do not know if I shall be able to keep the light burning,

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now, in the dim glow of the lighthouse’s dying lantern, as the wind howls like a wounded beast across the moor. The sea roars in defiance, and the sky is dark as a tomb. I fear the storm may claim me before dawn, and I would have one last word from a father’s heart before the world is swallowed by the night.

Five years have passed since you left this house, and five years have not brought a single letter, a single word, from you. I have tried to forgive you, but the silence has been a sharper blade than any I have ever known. You were my light, Eleanor, and now I am left in the dark, both in body and in soul.

I have always believed that a father’s love is eternal, and that no distance, no cruelty, can sever the bond between a parent and child. Yet you have chosen to walk away, and I have chosen to wait. I have tended the light for you, every night, as if your return might be the next dawn. I have whispered your name to the wind, hoping it might carry it to you.

But you have not come. And now, as the storm gathers, I wonder if I will ever see you again.

I have no regrets, child. I have lived my life in service to this light, to the sea, to the duty that binds me to this lonely rock. I have

My Dearest Eliza,

As I sit by the flickering light of the lighthouse, the wind howling like a banshee outside, I find myself compelled to write to you. It has been five long years since we last spoke, and though the distance between us has grown, I fear it is not the miles that have kept us apart, but the silence that has crept in like a shadow.

I have always believed that the sea holds secrets, and perhaps it is the sea that has whispered to me of your whereabouts. I have not seen you in years, not since you left with that man in the summer of '98. I remember the day as if it were yesterday. You were so full of fire and determination, your eyes alight with dreams that seemed as vast as the horizon. But the sea is fickle, and I fear that the tides have carried you away from the life you once knew.

Tonight, a storm is coming—one of those rare tempests that even the most seasoned sailors speak of in hushed tones. The sky is a canvas of dark clouds, and the wind has grown restless, as though it senses the chaos to come. I have been preparing for this for days, ensuring the lighthouse is secure, the beacon burns bright, and the lanterns are all in place. But I cannot shake the feeling that this storm may be more than a test of my resolve. It may be a reckoning.

**My Dearest Clara,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee outside these ancient stone walls. The sea is restless, and the storm is coming—great, black waves clawing at the cliffs, the sky a bruised violet, and the lighthouse lantern flickering like a dying soul. I have not spoken to you in five years, not a word, not a letter. But tonight, as the world seems to be ending, I feel the weight of silence pressing heavier than the storm.

You were a child when I last saw you, a little girl with eyes like the sea at dawn. I remember how you used to run up the steps of this lighthouse, your laughter echoing through the stone, and how you would press your small hand into mine, as if to steady me against the chaos of the world. You were my light, Clara, and I was the fool who let it go.

I have not written to you because I thought I was protecting you. I thought the world was too cruel, too full of sorrow, for a child like you. I thought the sea would swallow me whole before it could swallow you. But now, as I stand here, the storm raging around me, I wonder if I have been a fool in more ways than one.

My dear Eliza,

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a beast at the door, and the sea roaring as if it too has been wronged. The lamps have been lit, the lenses turned, and I’ve spent the last hour pacing the tower, trying to stave off the madness that comes with solitude. But tonight, the storm is not just a force of nature—it is a reckoning. And I fear I may not live to see the dawn.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years, and still I do not know what drove you away. You were once the light of my life, the one who made the long nights bearable, who laughed with me in the rain and sang to the waves. Now, I am left with only the sea and the ghosts of our conversations.

I’ve tried to write to you, more than once. I’ve filled pages with words that never reached you, and I’ve burned them, too, for fear that they might remind me of what I’ve lost. I told myself I’d wait for you to come back, that I’d keep the light burning for you, that I’d never let the sea take you. But you didn’t come, and I didn’t let it take me—not yet.

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with longing, for I have not held your hand in five long years, nor heard your voice in the stillness of the night. The sea has been a faithful companion, ever constant, yet it cannot replace the warmth of your presence. Tonight, as the storm gathers strength beyond the horizon, I find myself gripped by a fear that has not troubled me since the day you left.

You were but a girl when you ran from our home, and I, in my pride and folly, allowed it. I should have stood by you, not turned my back. I have spent these years in regret, watching the waves crash against these stone walls, wondering if you still remember the sound of my voice or the scent of the sea that once carried you away.

The wind howls now, and the sky is black as a stormy night. I have lit the lantern, as I always do, and the light cuts through the darkness like a beacon. It is a cruel irony, is it not? That I, who have spent my life guiding ships through treacherous waters, should find myself lost in the very thing I have always fought against—loneliness.

I have no doubt that you have built a life of your own, perhaps a life of comfort and ease, while I remain here, a solitary sentinel of the sea. But I wonder, dear Eleanor, if you ever looked back,

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now with a quill that trembles in my hand, not from the cold, but from the weight of my heart. The wind howls like a banshee through the cliffs, and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very land whole. I fear we may not see the dawn. But I write, for I know you are not here to hear the storm, and I must speak before it is too late.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and though the years have worn upon me, they have not worn the memory of you from my soul. I have watched the waves for countless nights, and in their rhythm, I have found solace—until now. Tonight’s storm is unlike any I have seen in my years here. The sky is black as ink, and the wind carries with it a fury that makes my bones shiver. I have lit the lantern, as I always do, and I pray the light holds against the gales, for it is the only thing that keeps the ships from crashing upon these rocks.

You were always a spirited child, Eleanor. I remember the day you first came to visit, with your father’s coat upon your back and your eyes alight with the fire of youth. **Blackmoor Head Lighthouse**  
**Northumberland, England**  
**April 12th, 1878**

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write to you now with a heart both heavy and full, for the winds have risen with a fury that has not been seen in these parts for many a year. The sea, that ever-faithful companion of my life, has turned upon me with a wrath I have long feared. The storm is upon us, and I do not know if I shall see the dawn.

Five years have passed since our last meeting, and I fear the silence between us has grown like the tide, rising higher each day. I have longed for your company, Eliza, yet the distance between us has only grown. You have not written, nor have you sent word of your own. I have heard of your marriage, of your life in London, of your children, and I have only the faintest glimmer of your thoughts. I have tried to write, but your address has changed, and I have heard that you now reside in the city, far from the sea and the solitude I have known for so many years.

I have always believed that the sea is a great teacher, and that in its waves and winds, one may find both comfort and warning. It has been my companion,

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. The wind howls like a beast outside, and the sea roars with fury, as if the very world is crying out for something I cannot give. But I must speak, for I fear the storm may take me before I can say what I have long kept locked within.

Five years have passed since you left, and I have not spoken your name in all that time. Not once. Yet I have not forgotten you, my dear. I have not forgotten the way your eyes would light up at the sight of the lighthouse, or how you would run to the shore with the tide, barefoot and laughing, as if the sea were a friend. I have not forgotten the sound of your voice, nor the warmth of your touch, nor the way you would sit beside me in the evenings, listening to the waves and dreaming of a future we might share.

But you chose your path, and I chose mine. We both did. I cannot undo what has been done, nor can I take back the words I said in anger, the coldness that crept into our home, the silence that grew like a wall between us. I have been a fool, Eliza. I have been a poor father, and I have been a man who failed to understand the depth of your heart.

**Letter to My Daughter**

*April 3rd, 1887*

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with a trembling hand, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside the lighthouse. The sea roars, and the waves seem to rise with a fury that defies nature itself. I have kept the light burning for hours now, and I pray it will guide some poor soul to safety before the storm claims them. But I fear that even the light may not be enough.

You were never meant to be raised by a man like me. I know that. I have always been a shadow of a man—stiff, unfeeling, and more at home with the sea than with the warmth of a hearth. You were a child of light, Eleanor. I was a child of darkness. And I failed you.

Five years have passed since the last time we spoke, and I have spent those years in silence, haunted by the words I could not say. I remember the last time we saw each other, standing at the edge of the dock, the rain falling in sheets, your eyes full of questions I could not answer. You asked if I loved you, and I did not know how to say it. I thought I had already said it, long ago, in the way I carried you when you were small, in the way I watched over you as you grew. But perhaps I was wrong.

I have always been a lighthouse

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the ink of a man who has long since lost the use of his hands, and with the heart of a father who has long since lost the use of his voice. The wind howls like a wounded beast outside these stone walls, and the sea roars as though it would swallow the lighthouse whole. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet tonight, as the storm gathers its strength, I feel the weight of silence heavier than any storm.

You were twelve when you left. A child, full of fire and fury, and I, a man too proud to say the words that would have kept you. I told myself it was for your own good. That the world was too cruel, too vast, and that I could not protect you from it. But I was wrong, Eleanor. I was so very wrong.

I have spent these years watching the sea, not with the eyes of a man, but with the eyes of a father. I have watched the waves crash against the rocks, and I have wondered if they are like the tears I have wept in the dark, unspoken and unremembered. I have watched the light flicker, and I have wondered if it is like the light of your smile, which once lit up my nights more than any beacon ever could.

You were not meant to be alone, Eleanor. You were not meant to carry the weight of the world on your shoulders

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not find you at all. I write with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside this old stone tower, and the sea roiling with fury. Tonight, a storm is coming—one that may not spare this lighthouse or the lives of those who dwell within it. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet, as I sit here by the light, I find myself thinking of you more than ever.

I have not written to you because I feared the words would not be enough. I have not written because I feared you would not read them, or worse, that you would read them and feel the weight of my silence. You were always the daughter who asked questions, who sought answers beyond what I could give. I was not a man of great words, but I was a man of duty, and I believed that duty meant keeping the light burning, even when the world outside seemed to fade into darkness.

You were young when you left, not in age, but in spirit. You were always chasing something beyond the horizon, always looking for a future that did not include me. I did not understand then, and I still do not. But I know this: you were never meant to stay here with me. You were meant for the world beyond, for the grandeur of life, for the promise of something greater than the sea.

I write to you from the very heart of the storm, where the sea roars like a beast in pain and the wind howls with a voice that sounds like the very soul of the earth itself. The waves rise and fall with a fury that makes my bones tremble, and I have not yet seen the dawn. Five years have passed since you left, and I have counted every day, every hour, every minute of silence. I have not spoken your name aloud since the day you walked out of our home with a heart as cold as the northern winds that howl across these cliffs. I have not spoken to you, nor have I dared to think of you. I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. **Letter to My Daughter**

My Dearest Eliza,

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not be the first I’ve written to you in five long years. I’ve been meaning to write, to put words to the silence that has stretched between us like the vast, unbroken sea, but the weather has been unkind, and my hands have been too steady with salt and worry to hold a pen.

You remember the night I left, don’t you? The night I chose the lighthouse over the life we once shared. I told myself it was for you, that the sea was a safer place, a more certain one. But I was wrong. I’ve spent every day since in the company of waves, wind, and the endless, indifferent sky. I’ve watched the horizon with more than just duty in my heart, and I’ve come to understand that some things are not meant to be tamed.

Tonight, the storm is coming. The air has been heavy with the scent of rain, and the wind howls like a beast in the night. I’ve seen the sea grow restless, and I know it will not be gentle. I’ve lit the lamp, as I have done every night since the day you left, and I’ve told myself that if I must go, I will go with the light still burning. But I fear the light may not be enough.

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**April 13th, 1897**

**The Lighthouse, Isle of Skye**

I sit here by the window, the wind howling like a banshee, and I feel the weight of the sea pressing against the stone walls. The lantern is lit, as it must be, though I fear the light may not be enough to guide the lost souls who will come seeking shelter in the dark. The storm is coming, and I am not certain I will see the dawn.

Five years have passed since you left, and yet the memory of your face haunts me more than the ghost of the sea. I have been a lighthouse keeper for thirty years, and I have seen many storms, many ships swallowed by the abyss. But none have ever felt as cruel as the silence you left behind.

I have tried to write to you, Eleanor. I have filled page after page with words that would have made you proud—words of duty, of sacrifice, of the life I have built here. But when I finally found the courage to send them, the postman said the house where you once lived had been sold. The new owners were strangers, and the letters were returned to me with no explanation. I have no idea where you are now, and I have no idea if you even want to hear from me.
